title: Why LeNet Still Matters
concept: LeNet still matters because it established the basic CNN blueprint—learn local features, shrink and deepen them, then classify—which later families like AlexNet expanded to larger data, larger models, and harder image tasks.

exercises.prompt: If a design becomes a blueprint, what usually happens next?
exercises.explain: Right — later builders keep the core idea and expand it for bigger goals.

options.text: Later systems reuse the core pattern and scale it up
It stops mattering the day after it appears

cards.title: Old blueprint, modern echoes
cards.body: LeNet is not important only because it came early. It is important because later CNN families kept reusing its big idea: stack learned visual stages so features become richer before a classifier makes the final call.

cards.title: What LeNet passed forward
cards.body: LeNet helped establish a core CNN recipe: local filters, progressive feature extraction, shrinking steps, and a classifier head. Later models changed the scale, depth, activations, and training tricks, but that structural logic stayed recognizable.

cards.title: Cottage to skyscraper
cards.body: A small early building can still teach the blueprint for bigger ones. LeNet is like a sturdy first design whose floor plan later architects enlarged into taller, busier, and more powerful structures.

cards.title: Same recipe, bigger mission
cards.body: AlexNet later attacked a much larger color-image problem than LeNet did, but it still relied on stacked convolutional stages and a classifier head. The family resemblance matters even when the scale changes a lot.

cards.title: Still matters does not mean still state-of-the-art
cards.body: LeNet matters as a design ancestor, not because it beats modern vision models. The point is to understand the recipe it proved, so later architectures feel like evolutions instead of random inventions.

cards.title: From LeNet to the next wave
cards.body: Later CNN families often look like scaled-up descendants rather than unrelated creatures.

left.text:
Conv stages learn features
Pooling/shrinking reduces size
Dense head picks class

right.text:
More data, more depth, more channels
Harder color-image tasks
Same basic CNN logic grows up

exercises.prompt: Why does LeNet still matter in a course on CNN architectures?
exercises.explain: Exactly — it gave later families a working structural recipe, even though they later grew much larger.

options.text: Because it established a CNN blueprint that later architectures expanded
Because later CNNs stopped using convolutions entirely
Because it solved every vision problem so no other architecture was needed
Because it replaced all classifier heads with handwritten rules

exercises.prompt: True or false: Later CNNs such as AlexNet can be understood partly as larger, tougher versions of the same general recipe LeNet helped establish.
exercises.explain: True — later models changed the scale and tricks, but the family resemblance is real.
Blueprint is right — LeNet's historical power is that it gave later CNNs a design pattern to grow from.
exercises.sentence: LeNet still matters because it served as a CNN ___ for later architectures.

exercises.prompt: Which bridge best leads from LeNet into AlexNet?
exercises.explain: Right — AlexNet makes more sense when you see it as a scaled-up next step, not a totally unrelated invention.

options.text: LeNet showed the basic CNN recipe on digits, and AlexNet later pushed a bigger version of that recipe onto harder large-scale image data
LeNet and AlexNet are unrelated because one uses images and the other does not
AlexNet matters only because it removed the need for convolutional stages

exercises.prompt: You are about to start AlexNet. Which summary best uses LeNet as the handoff point?
exercises.explain: Exactly. LeNet proved the core CNN recipe on a smaller digit task. AlexNet is the next big step: a larger, more powerful version aimed at much harder large-scale image recognition.

options.text: LeNet proved the basic conv-and-pooling recipe could work; AlexNet will show what happens when that idea scales to bigger data and harder images
LeNet matters only as a museum piece, so AlexNet starts from completely unrelated principles
AlexNet is mainly a lesson about replacing CNNs with handwritten rules
LeNet already solved large natural-image recognition, so AlexNet adds nothing new
